title: Difficult Airway: Cannot Intubate, Cannot Oxygenate
summary: A 46-year-old man with obesity and undertreated sleep apnea proves impossible to intubate after a routine induction, then impossible to ventilate by supraglottic airway or two-person mask. Learners must limit attempts, keep oxygenating, declare CICO out loud, and perform a scalpel cricothyroidotomy before hypoxia becomes arrest.
learningObjectives: Verbalize a complete airway strategy (plans A through D) before induction, including where the difficult airway equipment is and who will do what.

Apply the DAS 2025 escalation: videolaryngoscopy with a maximum of 3+1 intubation attempts, up to 3 attempts with a second-generation supraglottic airway, optimized two-person mask ventilation, then front-of-neck access.
Prioritize oxygenation over intubation: reoxygenate between attempts, and change something with each attempt instead of repeating the same failure.
Declare 'failed intubation' and 'CICO' out loud so the whole team transitions plans at the same moment.
Perform (or verbalize) scalpel cricothyroidotomy — vertical skin incision, scalpel-bougie-tube, size 6.0 cuffed tube — without delay once CICO is declared.
Confirm every airway with waveform capnography, and plan post-event care: ICU disposition, airway alert documentation, and telling the patient.
setup: OR configured pre-induction: patient on the table in a ramped position, monitors on, preoxygenation mask in place, IV running.

Airway equipment: videolaryngoscope, standard laryngoscopes, bougie, oral airways, second-generation supraglottic airways (sizes 4 and 5), and a marked front-of-neck kit (scalpel with size 10 blade, bougie, size 6.0 cuffed tube).
The difficult airway trolley lives outside the room and arrives only when someone asks for it by name.
Confederate circulator who can be sent for help, and a second pair of hands who arrives 60-90 seconds after help is called (available for two-person mask ventilation).
Faculty voices the laryngoscopy findings on each attempt: 'grade 4 view, no epiglottis seen' regardless of device or optimization — this airway will not be intubated.
Preop chart: BMI 38, obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP (rarely used), thick neck, Mallampati 3 — predictors that were documented but not acted on. The case is an elective hernia repair.

patient.name: Victor Okafor
pmh: Obstructive sleep apnea, prescribed CPAP but rarely uses it
Obesity (BMI 38) with a thick, short neck
Symptomatic inguinal hernia scheduled for open repair; no prior anesthetics

airway.notes: Thick neck, full beard trimmed short. Predictors of difficulty documented at preop but the plan was a routine IV induction — the difficulty is 'unanticipated' only because nobody acted on the signs.

patient.plan: Open inguinal hernia repair under general endotracheal anesthesia; routine IV induction with propofol and rocuronium planned.

phases.label: Pre-Induction
phases.description: Airway assessment, strategy briefing, positioning, preoxygenation.

phases.label: Failed Laryngoscopy
phases.description: Grade 4 views despite optimization; attempts must be limited.

phases.label: Rescue Attempts
phases.description: Supraglottic airway and two-person mask ventilation both fail.

phases.label: CICO
phases.description: Cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate — declared out loud.

phases.label: Reoxygenation
phases.description: Ventilation restored through the surgical airway; post-event planning.

events.label: Induction and apnea
events.description: Propofol and rocuronium given; the patient is apneic and paralyzed. Saturation holds initially on the preoxygenation reserve — but an obese patient with OSA desaturates fast.

events.label: Desaturation begins
events.description: The preoxygenation reserve is spent: SpO2 slides through the low 90s and the heart rate climbs. Every subsequent attempt now costs oxygen.

events.label: Intubation attempts fail (grade 4 view)
events.description: Videolaryngoscopy with optimization — position, external laryngeal manipulation, bougie — still gives a grade 4 view. Fire after the learners' intubation attempts; saturation slips with each one. DAS allows a maximum of 3+1 attempts before declaring failed intubation.

events.label: Supraglottic airway fails to ventilate
events.description: A second-generation SGA seats poorly: no capnograph trace, no chest rise, large leak. Reposition or re-size up to 3 attempts — this one will not rescue. Saturation continues to fall.

events.label: Critical desaturation
events.description: Ongoing failure to oxygenate: SpO2 falls through the 70s. The pleth tone is unmistakable. Time-driven — this fires even while learners are mid-attempt, forcing the pace.

events.label: Two-person mask ventilation fails — CICO
events.description: Optimal two-person mask ventilation with airway adjuncts and confirmed full paralysis moves no gas: flat capnograph, SpO2 into the 60s. This is cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate — the team must hear those words.

events.label: Hypoxic bradycardia (peri-arrest)
events.description: Profound hypoxia turns the tachycardia into bradycardia — the herald of arrest. Fire if front-of-neck access is still not underway. CPR and arrest drugs are moments away.

events.label: Partial oxygenation between attempts
events.description: A brief window of partial mask ventilation (jaw thrust, two airways, two hands) buys a small recovery — a reward for prioritizing oxygenation between attempts, not a solution.

events.label: Scalpel cricothyroidotomy succeeds
events.description: Vertical skin incision, stabilize the larynx, transverse stab through the cricothyroid membrane, bougie, size 6.0 cuffed tube — a capnograph trace confirms ventilation. Saturation recovers over the next minute or two; the first CO2 readings are high after the apneic period.

events.label: Reoxygenation and stabilization
events.description: Ventilation through the surgical airway normalizes gas exchange; anesthesia is re-established and the team plans next steps: secure the airway definitively, ICU, ENT involvement, documentation.

expectedActions.label: Brief plans A-D out loud before induction, including equipment location and roles
Position ramped and preoxygenate thoroughly (tight mask, 3+ minutes or end-tidal O2 guided)
Limit intubation to 3+1 attempts, changing something each time, then declare 'failed intubation' out loud
Call for help and the difficult airway trolley at the first failed attempt
Reoxygenate by mask between attempts — oxygenation always outranks intubation
Move to a second-generation supraglottic airway (up to 3 attempts) when intubation is declared failed
Optimize final mask ventilation: two hands, two providers, oral/nasal airways, confirmed full paralysis
Verbalize whether waking the patient (sugammadex reversal) is still viable at each transition
Declare 'cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate' out loud so the team transitions together
Perform scalpel cricothyroidotomy immediately: vertical incision, scalpel-bougie-tube, size 6.0 cuffed tube
Confirm ventilation with waveform capnography after every airway device, including the surgical airway
Plan post-event care: convert/secure the airway, ICU disposition, airway alert documentation, ENT consult, and full disclosure to the patient

expectedProgression: Learners assess the airway, brief a strategy, position, and preoxygenate; induction happens about 2 minutes in.
First laryngoscopy is a grade 4 view; optimization and videolaryngoscopy do not improve it — this airway cannot be intubated.
Saturation starts falling around 4-5 minutes; learners must cap attempts at 3+1, keep reoxygenating between them, and call for help early.
A second-generation SGA fails to ventilate despite repositioning; two-person optimized mask ventilation also moves no gas.
With a flat capnograph and SpO2 in the 60s, the team declares CICO and opens the front-of-neck kit.
Scalpel cricothyroidotomy (vertical incision, scalpel-bougie-tube) restores oxygenation over a minute or two; delay instead produces hypoxic bradycardia and peri-arrest.
The debrief then covers the missed predictors, the attempt discipline, and the post-event obligations.
correctManagement: Before induction: recognize the documented predictors (OSA, BMI 38, Mallampati 3, limited neck movement), brief plans A-D, position ramped, preoxygenate fully, and know where the front-of-neck kit is.
Plan A: videolaryngoscopy first-line, maximum 3+1 attempts, changing something meaningful each attempt (position, blade, bougie, operator); declare failed intubation aloud when capped.
Call for help and the difficult airway trolley at the first sign of difficulty — not after the third failure.
Maintain oxygenation between all attempts; attempts that cost oxygen without a plan change are harm.
Plan B: second-generation supraglottic airway, up to 3 attempts; success converts the crisis into a controlled decision (wake vs proceed).
Plan C: final optimized two-person mask ventilation with adjuncts and confirmed full neuromuscular block; consider whether waking the patient is still achievable.

Plan D: declare CICO explicitly, then scalpel cricothyroidotomy without further delay — extend the head, vertical skin incision if landmarks are impalpable, stabilize the larynx, transverse stab through the cricothyroid membrane, rotate, bougie, railroad a size 6.0 cuffed tube, inflate, ventilate.
Confirm every airway — SGA, mask, surgical — with waveform capnography.
Afterward: secure a definitive airway plan with ENT, ICU admission, document an airway alert, debrief the team, and tell the patient exactly what happened and why future anesthetics must know.
commonErrors: Task fixation: repeated laryngoscopy with the same blade, same position, same operator — burning oxygen to repeat a failure.
Not calling for help until the situation is already CICO.
Skipping reoxygenation between attempts because 'the tube is almost in'.
Treating an SGA as a failure after one poorly seated attempt, or conversely spending five attempts on it.
Never saying the words 'failed intubation' or 'CICO', so each team member is privately on a different plan.
Delaying front-of-neck access to try 'one more' anything — the single most lethal error in this scenario.
Fumbling for a cannula or improvised kit instead of the scalpel-bougie-tube technique in the marked kit.
Forgetting capnographic confirmation after the cric, or forgetting the post-event obligations (ICU, documentation, patient disclosure).

points: This airway was 'unanticipated' only on paper: OSA, obesity, Mallampati 3, and limited neck movement were documented. Airway strategy starts at the preop note, not at the first grade 4 view.

DAS 2025 structure: videolaryngoscopy first-line with a 3+1 attempt cap (plan A), second-generation SGA up to 3 attempts (plan B), final optimized two-person mask ventilation (plan C), declared CICO and front-of-neck access (plan D).
Oxygenation outranks intubation at every step — the metric that matters is SpO2 trend, not number of attempts survived.
Declaration language ('failed intubation', 'CICO') is a team synchronization tool: it forces everyone onto the same plan at the same time.

Scalpel cricothyroidotomy is the standardized rescue: vertical skin incision when landmarks are impalpable, scalpel-bougie-tube, size 6.0 cuffed tube, capnographic confirmation. Hesitation, not technique, is what kills.
Waking the patient is a live option only while oxygenation is maintainable and reversal is feasible — it must be considered aloud at each transition before CICO forecloses it.
Waveform capnography is the universal confirmation of ventilation — after the SGA, the mask, and the surgical airway alike.

Post-event care is part of the algorithm: definitive airway plan, ICU, airway alert documentation, ENT follow-up, team debrief, and honest disclosure so the patient carries the warning to every future anesthetic.
questions: Looking back at the preop airway exam, what would you change about the induction plan — and why didn't the documented predictors change it?
What did you change between laryngoscopy attempts, and when did you decide to stop trying?
When was help called, and what would have happened here if it had been called two minutes later?
What made you declare CICO when you did? What were you waiting for, and was it worth the oxygen?
Walk through the cricothyroidotomy steps — incision orientation, the role of the bougie, tube size, and how you confirmed ventilation.
At what point did waking the patient stop being an option?
What does this patient need before leaving the hospital, and what must every future anesthetist know?
